skill.name: multi-agent-patterns
skill.description: Guides users through multi-agent architecture patterns, including supervisors, swarms, hierarchies, handoffs, context isolation, consensus, and failure handling.
skill.summary: Design multi-agent systems with clear coordination, context boundaries, and recovery patterns.
skill.author: muratcankoylan

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported command execution, weak cryptography, sensitive file, filesystem, and network indicators. Review found the command, cryptography, sensitive file, and network findings are false positives from markdown examples, prose, diagrams, URLs, and ordinary Python identifiers. The only confirmed concern is illustrative filesystem code that should be adapted with path validation before production use.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fence False Positives
low_findings.description: Static detections for Ruby or shell backtick execution are markdown fences, diagrams, and code examples. They do not execute commands, and scripts/coordination.py contains no shell invocation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are markdown code fences or example blocks. The executable Python script does not import subprocess, os.system, eval, or related command execution APIs.

low_findings.title: Documentation Links Flagged as Network Activity
low_findings.description: The network indicators are external documentation links and one function definition line. No HTTP client call or data exfiltration path was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are cited as references for LangGraph, AutoGen, CrewAI, and an arXiv paper. There is no runtime code that opens these URLs or transmits local data.

low_findings.title: Illustrative File Writes Need Safe Adaptation
low_findings.description: The reference material includes examples that write shared state, lock files, and checkpoints. These examples are not executed by the skill, but production users should add path validation and robust locking.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are visible in code examples and use caller-provided workspace or checkpoint paths. The risk is limited because they are documentation snippets, not active marketplace execution paths.

low_findings.title: Weak-Crypto and Sensitive-File Alerts Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: Static weak cryptography and certificate or key alerts are lexical matches in prose, uuid usage, and a keys() call. No cryptographic algorithm, secret file read, certificate handling, or credential access was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations contain descriptive text, uuid import for message IDs, and dictionary key iteration. I did not find evidence of MD5, SHA1, private key handling, or certificate file access.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unvalidated Example File Paths
dangerous_patterns.description: The filesystem coordination examples build paths from workspace, key, resource, and workflow identifiers. If copied into production, these inputs should be constrained to a trusted directory.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The snippets show direct string interpolation into filesystem paths. They are examples rather than active code, so exploitation depends on a user adopting them without additional safeguards.

content.value_statement: Complex agent tasks often exceed one context window and lose coordination quality. This skill explains architecture patterns, handoff protocols, consensus methods, and failure controls.
actual_capabilities: Explains supervisor, swarm, and hierarchical multi-agent architectures.
Shows how context isolation changes task decomposition and delegation.
Describes handoff protocols for moving work between specialized agents.
Covers consensus methods, including weighted voting and debate.
Identifies common failure modes and practical mitigations.
Provides reference examples for LangGraph, AutoGen, and CrewAI-style designs.
limitations: It provides architecture guidance, not a complete production framework.
Example snippets require adaptation, validation, and testing before deployment.
It does not select models, tools, or infrastructure automatically.
It does not guarantee lower cost or latency than a single-agent design.

use_cases.title: Plan a Research Agent Team
use_cases.description: Design separate research, analysis, fact-checking, and writing agents with clear supervisor routing.

use_cases.title: Improve Agent Handoffs
use_cases.description: Define state transfer rules so agents can pass work without losing task context.

use_cases.title: Review Multi-Agent Failure Modes
use_cases.description: Check an existing design for bottlenecks, divergence, error propagation, and runaway coordination cost.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose between supervisor, swarm, and hierarchical patterns for this task: [describe task]. Explain the tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Design Handoffs
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a handoff protocol for agents that handle [domain]. Include transferred state, success criteria, and failure handling.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Coordination Risk
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this multi-agent architecture for bottlenecks, divergence, consensus failure, and context leakage: [paste design].

prompt_templates.title: Specify Production Controls
prompt_templates.prompt: Create production controls for this multi-agent system: [paste workflow]. Include time limits, retries, checkpoints, observability, and validation.

output_examples.input: I need a multi-agent research workflow for market analysis.
output_examples.output: A supervisor routes work to research, analysis, validation, and writing agents. Each agent receives a scoped task and returns a compact result.

output_examples.input: Our agents keep agreeing with wrong answers.
output_examples.output: Use debate rounds and weighted confidence instead of simple majority voting. Require each agent to state evidence and critique alternatives.

output_examples.input: A swarm design keeps drifting from the user request.
output_examples.output: Add explicit objective boundaries, convergence checks, and time-to-live limits. Keep a shared state summary for handoffs.

best_practices: Use multi-agent designs only when task decomposition clearly improves context quality or parallelism.
Define handoff payloads, output schemas, and validation rules before adding more agents.
Test failure scenarios, including stalled agents, incorrect results, and repeated handoffs.
anti_patterns: Do not create many agents only to mirror an organization chart.
Do not let every agent see all context when isolation is the main goal.
Do not rely on majority voting when weaker agents can reinforce wrong answers.

faq.question: When should I use this skill?
faq.answer: Use it when a task needs multiple specialized agents, context isolation, parallel subtasks, or explicit coordination protocols.

faq.question: Does this skill run agents automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides architecture guidance, examples, and design patterns for implementing multi-agent systems.

faq.question: Which frameworks does it discuss?
faq.answer: It references LangGraph, AutoGen, and CrewAI-style approaches as examples of common implementation patterns.

faq.question: Can it help reduce token usage?
faq.answer: Not always. It explains that multi-agent systems often use more tokens and should be justified by better decomposition or parallel work.

faq.question: Does it cover security concerns?
faq.answer: It covers operational failure modes such as divergence, error propagation, bottlenecks, retries, and validation between agents.

faq.question: Can I copy the code examples directly?
faq.answer: Treat the examples as starting points. Add path validation, access controls, observability, and tests before production use.
